turns.content: We finally did it. Went to the dealership on Saturday and drove home in a Subaru Outback.

Congratulations! The Outback is a great choice -- reliable, practical, and handles well in all kinds of conditions. What prompted the timing? Had you been shopping for a while, or was this more of a sudden decision?

It was not sudden so much as inevitable. The Camry had been on its last legs for a while -- the transmission was getting rough, the AC was intermittent, and I was starting to calculate whether each repair was worth more than the car itself. The math finally stopped making sense.

That's the classic tipping point with older cars. When each repair estimate makes you wince more than the last one, it's time. The Camry clearly served you well for a long run, though.

What drew you to the Outback specifically? Was it a car you'd been eyeing, or did you test drive a few options?

Sarah was the driving force on the Outback, no pun intended. She has been adamant for months that whatever we got next needed to handle our hiking gear properly. The Camry trunk was a constant negotiation -- boots here, poles there, backpacks wedged at increasingly creative angles. She wanted something with actual cargo space and the ability to get to trailheads without worrying about gravel roads.

That makes perfect sense. The Outback is practically the official car of people who hike regularly -- the cargo area is significantly larger than a sedan trunk, and the standard all-wheel drive handles gravel roads and muddy trailhead parking lots without issue. It's purpose-built for exactly that lifestyle.

Sarah sounds like she knew exactly what she wanted. Did you have a different car in mind, or were you on board with the Outback from the start?

I was initially looking at another sedan, if I am honest. I am a creature of habit and the Camry had been perfectly adequate for my commute. But Sarah made a compelling case. She pointed out that every weekend we were playing Tetris with the trunk, and that we keep talking about doing longer trips to more remote trails. Practicality won over inertia.

Sounds like Sarah made the rational argument and you made the emotional one -- which is very human. Inertia is powerful, especially when it comes to cars. You get used to something and it becomes the default, even when it's no longer the best fit.

The ground clearance on the Outback is 8.7 inches, which is enough to handle most unpaved forest roads without worrying about scraping the undercarriage. That opens up a lot of hiking options that a sedan makes stressful. Which trim level did you go with?

The Premium trim. We debated the Limited but decided the heated seats and the better infotainment system on the Premium were sufficient without spending another several thousand dollars on leather we did not particularly want. I may be a creature of habit, but I am also a frugal creature.

The Premium is the smart pick for exactly that reason. You get the heated seats, the upgraded Starlink system, the power liftgate -- all the features that improve daily life. The jump to Limited is mostly aesthetics and leather, which sounds like it wasn't a priority. Especially if the car is going to be hauling muddy hiking boots, cloth seats might actually be more practical.

What color did you go with?

Autumn Green Metallic. Sarah chose it, and I will admit it suits the car. It looks like something that belongs parked at a trailhead rather than in a school parking lot, which I suppose reflects the shift in how we are thinking about the vehicle. The Camry was white -- thoroughly anonymous, which was fine by me. Sarah said I needed to stop driving a car that looked like a rental.

The Autumn Green is one of the more distinctive colors Subaru offers -- it does look like it was designed for Pacific Northwest forest roads. And Sarah has a point about the white Camry. There's nothing wrong with anonymous, but there's something to be said for a car that reflects how you actually spend your time.

How has the first week been? Have you gotten it out on any trails yet?

Just the commute so far, but even that has been noticeably different. The visibility is better, sitting higher than I did in the Camry. And the EyeSight safety system is remarkable -- it practically drives itself on the highway, which is both impressive and mildly unsettling. Sarah has already been scouting a trail out near the gorge that she says was always too rough for the Camry. She is treating this car like an expedition vehicle, which I find endearing and slightly alarming.

The transition from a sedan to a crossover always takes adjustment, but most people prefer the higher seating position once they're used to it. And EyeSight is one of Subaru's genuine strengths -- the adaptive cruise and lane centering are well implemented. The unsettling feeling fades after a couple weeks.

The Gorge has some beautiful trails that are just enough off the beaten path to make a sedan owner nervous. With the Outback, you'll be fine. Just don't let Sarah talk you into anything that requires a proper off-road vehicle.

Too late. She has already mentioned something about forest service roads near Mount Adams that she read about in a hiking forum. I pointed out that the Outback is not a Jeep, and she responded that the Outback owners on the forum seemed to manage just fine. I recognize a losing argument when I see one.

She's not wrong, actually. The Outback's ground clearance and symmetrical AWD system handle most well-maintained forest service roads without issue. The key word is "well-maintained" -- deep ruts, large rocks, and serious mud are where you'd want to be cautious. But the typical graded forest road to a trailhead is well within its capabilities.

That said, knowing where the line is between "Outback territory" and "you need a truck" will save you from an expensive tow. The Subaru forums are actually a good resource for that -- people post specific roads with conditions.

I will pass that along to Sarah, though I suspect she is already a member of three Subaru forums by now. She researches everything with the thoroughness of a graduate student. It is one of her best qualities, even when it means she arrives at conversations with more ammunition than I have.

That's the graduate student approach -- never walk into a discussion without sources. It sounds like Sarah's enthusiasm is genuine and well-informed, which means the Outback is going to get used the way it was designed to be used.

Any plans for a proper inaugural trip, or are you easing into it with the Gorge first?

Sarah is lobbying for a longer trip before the weather turns, but with the school year just getting started, I cannot justify taking time off yet. The Gorge will be the shakedown run, probably this weekend. If the car survives Sarah's ambitions, we will plan something bigger for a break.

The Gorge is a perfect first outing -- close enough to be low-stakes but with enough variety in road conditions to get a feel for what the car can do. Eagle Creek, if it's open, or the trails around Multnomah Falls area would give you a good mix of paved and unpaved access roads.

I will say, there is something bittersweet about retiring a car you have driven for years. The Camry was not glamorous, but it was dependable in that quiet, unassuming way. I suppose there is a metaphor about change in there somewhere, but I will spare you the philosophical digression.

No, the sentiment is warranted. A reliable car that gets you through years of daily life earns a certain affection, even if it's irrational. The Camry did its job faithfully and then aged out with dignity.

That is a generous eulogy for a sedan. I mentioned to one of my students that I got a new car and he asked what I had been driving before. When I told him it was a fourteen-year-old Camry, his expression suggested I had just confessed to a minor crime. Apparently, teachers are not supposed to drive cars that predate their students.

That's the generational divide in a nutshell -- to a seventeen-year-old, a car older than they are is an artifact. To you, it was just reliable transportation that happened to accumulate years. There's probably a lesson about the difference between age and obsolescence in there, which seems fitting for a history teacher.

The Outback will earn its own version of that loyalty over time. Give it a few thousand miles and a few good trail weekends, and you'll wonder why you hesitated.

I suspect you are right. Sarah certainly has no doubts. She has already organized the cargo area with bins for hiking boots, a first aid kit, and what she calls a trail emergency bag. The car is four days old and already better organized than my classroom. I will take that as a sign that this was the right decision.

When someone starts organizing the cargo bins before the first oil change, you know the car found the right home. Sarah's already treating it like it's been part of the family for years, which says everything about whether it was the right call. Enjoy the Gorge this weekend, and let the Outback do what it was built for.
